incorrects.description: Student selected A) 3 × 10, which has 30 dots but different row and column structure

steps.text: That array has 30 dots, but look at the rows. The reference has 5 rows. When you rotate, the rows become columns.

incorrects.description: Student selected C) 10 × 3, which has 30 dots but different row and column structure

steps.text: This array has 30 dots, but the row and column counts are different. When you rotate 5 rows of 6, you get 6 rows of 5.

incorrects.description: Student selected D) 2 × 15, which has 30 dots but completely different structure

steps.text: That array has 30 dots, but rotating doesn't change the numbers that much. If you turn 5 rows of 6, the 5 and the 6 swap places.

incorrects.description: Student answered incorrectly. System models the correct answer.

params.description: Reference array rotates 90 degrees, transforming from 5 rows of 6 to 6 rows of 5.

steps.text: Let me show you how this works. When I rotate the reference array, the 5 rows become 5 columns. The 6 dots in each row become 6 rows. So 5 rows of 6 becomes 6 rows of 5. The answer is B) 6 × 5. That is the rotated version of the reference array.
